Black-zone tag: doctor did not listen / symptom dismissed
Documented examples: Endometriosis UK survey; PHSO family quote in scan case; Healthwatch waiting reports
Signals lost: pain severity, patient concern, duration
Likely mechanism: bias/minimization, time pressure, normal tests
Design implication: Capture patient concern verbatim; repeat concerns prompt review.

Documented examples: Jess's Rule origin story and many rare/chronic patient stories show unexplained symptoms attributed to benign causes
Signals lost: progressive symptoms, objective change, patient baseline
Likely mechanism: anchoring, demographic assumptions
Design implication: Require 'what else could this be?' differential when persistent symptoms recur.

Documented examples: Cancer safety-netting explicitly says monitor persistent symptoms after negative tests; NHS bowel screening says negative FIT does not rule out cancer if symptoms occur
Signals lost: ongoing symptoms despite normal lab/imaging/screen
Likely mechanism: test result treated as diagnosis closure
Design implication: Negative result should trigger symptom-status check and follow-up plan.

Where it appears: GP labs, radiology, screening, hospital discharge
Documented examples: CQC test result guidance; HSSIB radiology; PHSO scan failures; FQHC abnormal FIT navigation
Signals lost: abnormal value/finding, critical imaging, positive FIT
Likely mechanism: no owner; inbox/worklist failure; patient not notified
Design implication: Closed-loop result action with owner, due date, patient notification and escalation.

Where it appears: primary care to specialist, HWC to referral facility, FQHC to colonoscopy
Documented examples: Healthwatch waits; e-RS RAS; PRECISE/FQHC navigation; India HWC referral
Signals lost: referral sent but not accepted/booked/completed
Likely mechanism: waitlists, more-info loops, transport/cost barriers
Design implication: Track referral state as steps, not binary referred/not referred.

Documented examples: NHS 111 Directory of Services; e-RS redirect/return; UTC redirection
Signals lost: clinical fit to service
Likely mechanism: service directory mismatch; insufficient triage context
Design implication: Show confidence and alternatives; audit redirect failures and returns.

Black-zone tag: fragmented care / no one connected the dots
Where it appears: primary care repeat visits, ED returns, chronic disease
Documented examples: Brian HSSIB case; primary-care diagnostic error trigger studies; Israel/EMR caveat
Signals lost: repeated presentations, prior cancer, outside visits
Likely mechanism: multiple clinicians and systems; history not surfaced
Design implication: Episode-based timeline across channels and organizations.

Where it appears: telehealth, care navigation, ED/UC intake, low-resource algorithms
Documented examples: AHRQ telehealth, BMA navigation, UCA red flags, IMCI
Signals lost: family history, prior results, duration, red flags
Likely mechanism: short forms, role boundaries, patient language/literacy
Design implication: Progressive history capture: minimal fields + clinician review for uncertainty.

Documented examples: Brian case: prior breast cancer not integrated with repeated back pain pattern
Signals lost: prior cancer or high-risk condition, family history
Likely mechanism: record exists but not prominent in workflow
Design implication: High-risk history cards tied to current symptom categories.

Where it appears: primary care, HEARTS, HWC, urgent care
Documented examples: BP protocols, HWC NCD screening, urgent care vitals, CKD stories
Signals lost: worsening BP/eGFR/labs/vitals over time
Likely mechanism: single-visit view; no trend alert
Design implication: Trend graph + threshold crossing + overdue review.

Where it appears: urgent care registration, ED triage, CHW/IMCI
Documented examples: UCA red flags; ESI high-risk; IMCI/iCCM danger signs
Signals lost: chest pain, SOB, neurologic deficits, distress, danger signs
Likely mechanism: first handler non-clinical or undertrained; vague presentation
Design implication: Plain-language red-flag checklist with immediate escalation.

Documented examples: Jess's Rule; Brian HSSIB case; PHSO fifth A&E visit scan case
Signals lost: same symptom count and worsening
Likely mechanism: encounters treated as separate episodes
Design implication: Global repeat-presentation counter across channels and clinicians.

Where it appears: chronic/rare disease, referral waits, screening follow-up
Documented examples: NORD rare diagnosis, Endometriosis UK, Healthwatch, FQHC navigation
Signals lost: patient-chased results/referrals, symptom timeline
Likely mechanism: system lacks active owner or longitudinal synthesis
Design implication: Give patients shared timeline + escalation rights + navigator support.

Documented examples: CQC test results; ECRI testing errors; Brazil CHW relay
Signals lost: order reason, result action, CHW observation
Likely mechanism: free text lost; filing without action; specimen/order errors
Design implication: Structured fields for diagnostic intent, action and handoff.

Where it appears: ED discharge, radiology, e-RS, CHW referral
Documented examples: CDC pending tests; HSSIB radiology; HRSA continuity; WHO referral/counter-referral
Signals lost: responsibility and next step
Likely mechanism: delivery without acknowledgement/action
Design implication: Handoff = sender + receiver + action + deadline + closure.

Documented examples: CQC results, Cancer Research UK safety netting, NHS screening, PRECISE
Signals lost: who must call/act/book/monitor
Likely mechanism: task not assigned or patient expected to chase
Design implication: Every unresolved issue needs named owner and due date.

Documented examples: NORD rare diagnosis after decades; Denmark NSSC model tries to route nonspecific serious symptoms
Signals lost: non-specific clusters, unusual course
Likely mechanism: low prevalence; siloed specialists; anchoring
Design implication: Escalation pathway for unexplained multi-system symptoms and patient timeline import.

Documented examples: Endometriosis UK and CKD patient stories
Signals lost: pain/fatigue/symptom burden, functional impact
Likely mechanism: normal tests, stereotypes, communication failure
Design implication: Track function, duration and impact, not only test abnormality.

Documented examples: AHRQ PSNet telehealth; HSSIB GP online consultation
Signals lost: auscultation, palpation, neuro exam, private body area
Likely mechanism: remote modality limits
Design implication: Telehealth appropriateness rules and automatic face-to-face conversion.

Documented examples: HSSIB online consultation; NHS urgent care self-service
Signals lost: patients unable to use forms/kiosks; language/literacy
Likely mechanism: digital front door excludes or distorts input
Design implication: Maintain non-digital channels and assisted triage.

Documented examples: NHS bowel/breast/cervical pathways; CDC navigation
Signals lost: unreturned FIT, missed mammogram, missed colonoscopy
Likely mechanism: mail/outreach not enough; access barriers
Design implication: Funnel monitoring from invitation to diagnostic completion; navigation for barriers.

Documented examples: NHS outpatient advice/guidance and RAS
Signals lost: specialist advice but no appointment/test booked
Likely mechanism: advice substitutes for referral without safety net
Design implication: Every advice response should include failure criteria and follow-up owner.

Documented examples: Brazil FHS, India HWC, iCCM
Signals lost: home-observed symptoms, living conditions, adherence, danger signs
Likely mechanism: paper notes and informal relay not integrated
Design implication: Structured CHW-to-clinic handoff and shared registry.
